coredata.dc:title: P-doped carbon nano-powders for fingerprint imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Algarra, Manuel
Bartolić, Dragana
Radotić, Ksenija
Mutavdžić, Dragosav
Pino-González, Md. Soledad
Rodríguez-Castellón, Enrique
Lázaro-Martínez, Juan Manuel
Guerrero-González, Juan José
Esteves da Silva, Joaquim CG
Jiménez-Jiménez, José
coredata.dc:description: A simple, fast, and laboratory efficient doped P carbon nanoparticles synthesis is developed for fingerprint imaging, using 1,3-dihydroxyacetone and di-phosphorous pentoxide. Fluorescence nanoparticles, with an average size of 230 nm were obtained, without additional energy input or external heating. ATR, solid NMR, XPS and fluorescence spectroscopy revealed their surface functionalization; a reaction mechanism is proposed. Fluorescence measurements exhibited a maximum emission band at ca. 495 nm, when excited at 385 nm. The images obtained, on different surfaces such as mobile telephone screen, magnetic band and metallic surface of a credit card and a Euro banknote treated with the obtained nano-powders allows us to record positive matches, confirming that the experimental results illustrate the effectiveness of proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fingerprints
P-doped
DHA
Imaging
Nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of P Doped Carbon Nano Powders (P-CDs)
Characterization of P-CDs nano-powders. TEM and EDS analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology of the Republic of Serbia
ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors thank to the projects CTQ2015–68951-C3-3-R (MINECO, Spain), ANPCYT (PICT 2012-0151), Univ. Buenos Aires (UBACyT 2013–2016/043BA) and CONICET (PIP 2014–2016/130), and grant No 173017 ( Ministry of Education, Science and Technology of the Republic of Serbia ). It is acknowledged the support to COST (ACTION 16101). We also thank SCAI (Málaga, Spain) for performing the XPS and solid NMR measurements. MA thanks also ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação is also acknowledged through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-CQM+(Madeira 14–20 Program).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Survey TEM image of the obtained P-CDs nano-powders. (B) Magnification of a nanopowder.
ATR spectra obtained for P-CDs obtained by reaction of P2O5 with DHA.
(A) The survey XPS spectrum obtained for P-CDs (B) C 1 s, (C) O 1 s and (D) P 2p core levels.

13C CP-MAS (A) and 13C CPPI at a MAS rate of 15 kHz (B), 31P CP-MAS spectrum at a MAS rate of 10 kHz (C), 1H-MAS spectrum at a MAS rate of 30 kHz (D) and the 1H-dimension extracted from the 2D 1H–13C HETCOR experiment at 15 kHz with a contact time of 3 ms (E) for the P-CDs material.

(A) Excitation-emission landscape for the raw spectra of P-CDs and corresponding contour plot shown in inset; (B) Excitation spectrum of P-CDs for the emission at 480 nm (black) and matching emission spectrum (red) along with the estimated emission profiles for P-CDs at 416/446/495 nm (orange, green and blue).

Latent fingerprints images based on P-CDs nanoparticles on different surfaces of (A) LCD screen of a smartphone, (B) Metallic symbol of Visa credit card, (C) 20 Euro banknote and (D) Magnetic band of credit card.
AFIS analysis for fingerprints images developed using P-CDs nanoparticles (A) magnetic band and (B) LCD screen of a smartphone.
Proposed reactions for the DHA (I) in the generation of the products that cover the P-CDs nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: P-doped carbon nano-powders for fingerprint imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, fast, and laboratory efficient doped P carbon nanoparticles synthesis is developed for fingerprint imaging, using 1,3-dihydroxyacetone and di-phosphorous pentoxide. Fluorescence nanoparticles, with an average size of 230 nm were obtained, without additional energy input or external heating. ATR, solid NMR, XPS and fluorescence spectroscopy revealed their surface functionalization; a reaction mechanism is proposed. Fluorescence measurements exhibited a maximum emission band at ca. 495 nm, when excited at 385 nm. The images obtained, on different surfaces such as mobile telephone screen, magnetic band and metallic surface of a credit card and a Euro banknote treated with the obtained nano-powders allows us to record positive matches, confirming that the experimental results illustrate the effectiveness of proposed method.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprint is very well known when are considered one of the most important types of evidence in human identification. In general, three forms of fingerprint evidence that may be found at a crime scene are visible (or patent) prints, impression (or plastic) prints and latent prints [1,2]. Latent prints are not visible to the naked eye and thus require some means of development, their visualization with powder, or dusting, involves the application of finely divided particles that physically adhere to the aqueous and oily components in latent print residue on nonporous surfaces [3], being visualized via reflected light (light powders), absorbed light (dark powders), and luminescence (fluorescent powders). Usually, powdering, vacuum metal deposition; aqueous electrolytes, iodine fuming, ninhydrin, and cyanoacrylate fuming are widespread used for detection of fingerprint on various substrates [3–6].

In recent years, nanotechnology has offered nano-structured materials that possess several distinctive physical and chemical behavior such as noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) and quantum dots (QDs), which have been interested by researchers and industry because they can be employed in a broad spectrum of application fields including forensic fingerprints detection [7]. Multi-metal depositions (MMD) was the first use of metal NPs for fingerprints detection, performed by dissolved Au NPs and detected by Ag [8–11]. However, QDs have been used intensively from worldwide due to their several unique intrinsic optical and electronic properties [12], which make them potential candidates for optical detection. CdS-QDs was the first used for this purpose [13,14], followed by CdSe-QDs classified as n-type is mainly focused because of their tunable emission in the visible range [15,16]. Later, with the demand for more biocompatible QDs, cadmium-free QDs were introduced, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which constitute a promising type of small, biocompatible and multicolor luminescent nanoparticles, [17–22], with potential applications in a wide field such as bio-imaging [23,24], photo-catalysis [25], LED [26,27], energy conversion [28], sensing [29–31] and fluorescent inks [32]

In this paper, we report for the first time the preparation of phosphorous doped CQDs (P-CDs) nano-powders with an orange bright fluorescence under UV light, by a quickly and efficient method be used as fingerprints imaging. It is proposed a different one-step synthesis based on the reaction of P2O5 with a carbon source, dihydroxyacetone (DHA), to obtain carbon nanoparticles with a rich concentration of P heteroatoms in their composition, showing an alternative to the previous synthesis procedure to obtain P doped CQDs, such as the hydrothermal method using ATP, sucrose phosphate, diethylenetriaminepenta(methylenephosphonic acid) respectively and citric acid by reaction with phosphoric acid [33–37]. Throw the text will be justified the term carbon quantum dots (CQDs).
1,3-dihydroxyacetone dimer (DHA, 97%, Aldrich) and di-phosphorous pentoxide (P2O5, 97%, BDH) were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of P Doped Carbon Nano Powders (P-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of P-CDs was carried out with by reaction, at room temperature, between DHA (1 g) and P2O5 (0.163 g) molar ratio (10:1) by chemical mixing with a mortar. A viscous and brownish fluorescent powder was instantaneously produced and stored into a closed flask. After this was neutralized with CaO aqueous solution and lyophilized for 48 h v. Milli Q H2O, showing an orange luminescence under UV irradiation.

Zeta potential (ζ) of P-CDs was determined by DLS analysis, using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, U.K.) equipped with a 4 mW HeNe laser operating at λ = 633 nm. The ζ measurement were also performed at 25 °C in polycarbonate folded capillary cells, incorporated with Au plated electrodes (DTS1061) and deionized H2O was the dispersion medium. ζ was automatically obtained by the software, using the Stokes-Einstein and the Henry equation, with the Smoluchowski approximation. The morphology of P-CDs was analyzed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and examined under a FEI Talos F200X. XPS studies were acquired on a Physical Electronics PHI VersaProbe II spectrometer using monochromatic Al-Kα radiation (49.1 W, 15 kV and 1486.6 eV) for analyzing the core-level signals of the elements of interest with a hemispherical multichannel detector. The sample spectra were recorded with a constant pass energy value at 29.35 eV, using a 200 µm diameter circular analysis area. The X-ray photoelectron spectra obtained were analyzed using PHI SmartSoft software and processed using MultiPak 9.3 package. The binding energy values were referenced to adventitious carbon C 1 s signal (284.8 eV). Shirley-type background and Gauss-Lorentz curves were used to determine the binding energies. The ss-NMR experiments were performed at room temperature in a Bruker AVANCE-III HD 600 spectrometer equipped with a 3.2 or 2.5 mm MAS probe. The operating frequency for H, C and P was 600.09, 150.91 and 242.92 MHz, respectively. Glycine was used as an external reference for the 13C and 1H spectra and NH4H2PO4 was used as an external reference for the 31P spectrum. 31P and 13C CP-MAS (cross-polarization and magic angle spinning) spectra were recorded with 5 and 3 ms of contact time, respectively, with 1H decoupling during acquisition and a recycling time of 5 s. NMR experiments in solution were recorded in a Bruker Avance-400. Fluorescence spectra of P-CDs were collected using n Fl3–221 spectrofluorometer (JobinYvon Horiba, Paris, France) equipped with a 450 W Xe lamp and a photomultiplier tube. The spectrum of P-CDs was measured in the front-face configuration of the measuring cavity. The slits on the excitation and emission beams were fixed both at 4 nm. The spectra were corrected for dark counts. The Rayleigh masking was applied to reduce Rayleigh scattering from the solid sample, to improve sensitivity and accuracy of the measurement. For each compound a series of emission spectra was collected, by excitation at different wavelengths, thus obtaining excitation emission matrices (EEMs). The fluorescence of P-CDs was measured in the excitation wavelength 350–392 nm range. The emission spectra were measured in the range 405–680 nm, with 1 nm increment. The EEMs of P-CDs was analyzed by using Multivariate Curve Resolution-Alternating Least Squares (MCR-ALS) method [38,40], which extracted the number of components, as well as their emission profiles. All analyses were performed using The UNSCRAMBLER software package (Camo ASA). All measurements were performed at controlled temperature of 25 °C.

Multivariate Curve Resolution-Alternating Least Squares (MCR-ALS) is a chemometric method belonging to the factor analysis (FA) family of techniques [39]. MCR-ALS has become a popular chemometric method which is used for decomposition of the mixture signal (signal from multiple sources) into the signals of individual components. MCR-ALS can identify a model from the empirical data, without any a priori assumption about the nature or composition of the system under investigation. Successful decomposition of fluorescence spectra implies estimation of the number of sources of emitting photons (fluorophores), their emission and excitation profiles. Mathematically, MCR-ALS method can be expressed as bilinear model in the following way:
D = C
S
T
+ E
, where C is the matrix whose columns describe excitation profiles of fluorophores, with the number of rows which is equal to the number of excitation wavelengths (the number of emission spectra), and the number of columns is equal to the number of detected fluorophores. S T is the matrix whose rows are the emission spectra and the number of columns corresponds to the number of emission wavelengths. E is matrix of residuals. MCR-ALS is an algorithm that solves the MCR basic bilinear model using a constrained Alternating Least Squares algorithm. The constraints used to improve the interpretability of the profiles in C and S
T is non-negativity, uni-modality, closure, etc., [38,48].

The acquisitions of the fingerprints were acquired from the same volunteer. The fingerprints were obtaining by stamping onto the different selected surfaces, such as the smartphone screen, euro banknote, the magnetic band and the metallic surface of a credit card. The images of latent fingerprints were obtained using a Multi-zoom Nikon AZ-100 microscope and a Nikon Digital Sight-5Mc, connected to the microscope by a computer, in which the pictures were stored and visualized by Nis-Elements software. The microscope was equipped with an epifluorescence system, in which the samples were illuminated by a mercury lamp (100 W) and a variety of available filter blocks (UV-2A: EX 330–380; DM 400; BA 420) were used. Fingerprints recorded were analyzed to be validated by 3 M Cogent CAFIS system (3 M Cogent's Automated Fingerprint and Palm Print Identification System), a customizable software package that allows you to perform a wide range of tasks for processing, editing, searching, retrieving, and storing fingerprint images and subject records used by Scientific Police in Spain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of P-CDs nano-powders. TEM and EDS analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
P-CDs were obtained by one-step reaction, using P2O5 with DHA as source of carbon precursors. To our best knowledge, this is the first report of the preparation of P doped CQDs using this one-step method. At room temperature it leads at naked eyes an orange emission when the solid was irradiated under UV light. P-CDs showed a ζ (-17.5 mV), and further justified by XPS spectroscopy with the presence on the surface of polar groups (mainly –PO
4
3 −
groups). Several P2O5/DHA molar ratios (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 and 0.5) were tested, according with the fluorescence obtained when mixed at room temperature, finding 0.1 as the optimal ratio. Ratio > 0.1, not fluorescence was detected, and a carbonaceous material was obtained, and for lower ratios, the process was slowest, and it needs several days of reaction for detect the fluorescence. All further analysis and study was were performed with this stablished parameter.

To investigate more precisely the elemental composition and the uniformity of the chemical distribution, X-ray energy dispersion (EDS) spectroscopy was performed. It validated the presence of C (62.8 wt%), O (25.0 wt%) and P (12.2 wt%) as the main chemical components of P-CDs, in supplementary information is showed the EDS spectrum (Fig. SI1A). The EDS mapping images indicate the coexistence of C, O and P, all of them with uniform distribution among the whole nanoparticles (Fig. SI1B).

The resulting image from TEM ( Fig. 1A) revealed that these spherical nanoparticles were well separated from each other, favored by the higher surface negative charge as previously mentioned. The average particle size of the P-CDs ranges from 220 nm, obtained by DLS (Fig. SI2).

The surface chemical features of our P-CDs were analyzed by ATR, solid NMR and XPS spectroscopes. ATR ( Fig. 2), showed the characteristic stretching vibration mode υ(C˭O) at 1718 and 1636 cm−1 which confirmed the existence of carbonyl functional groups (ketone -C˭O and carboxylate -COO-) on their surface of P-CDs compared with the star material (DHA). Bands at approximately 2925 and 2892 cm−1 assigned to the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of CH2 groups, νa(CH2) and ν
s (CH2) respectively, accompanied by the corresponding δ(CH2) bending vibrations at around 1449, 1313 and 764 cm−1, ascribed to the aliphatic material. Two bands at 877/1077 cm−1 respectively, were attributed to–PO
4
3 −
stretching vibrations [41]. A weak band at 1210 cm−1 assigned to δ(OH) bending vibrations of hydroxyl groups. In Fig. SI3, supplementary information is compared the ATR spectra of DHA start material with the obtained as-P-CDs.
Raman and XRD spectroscopies confirmed the amorphous nature of P-CDs respectively (Figs. SI4–5). XRD pattern of the sample had a broad peak at about 20°, revealing amorphous carbon.

The XPS measurements were performed to determine the surface composition and the chemical state of the elements present in the P-CDs. The survey spectrum obtained is showed in Fig. 3 A, which clearly indicates the presence of C, P and O. High resolution XPS spectrum for C 1 s core energy level spectrum of P-CDs (Fig. 3B) is deconvoluted into four different contributions, corresponding to C-C/C˭C (adventitious carbon and graphitic/sp2 contributions) bonds at a binding energy of 284.8 eV (60.71%), C-OH and C-O-C at 286.4 eV(19.82%); C˭O bond at 287.9 eV (15.40%) and carboxylate at 289.1 eV (4.08%). Meanwhile the O 1 s (Fig. 3 C) can deconvolute into two curves attributed to COOH at 531.8 eV (19.74%) and for –PO
4
3 −
at 533.1 eV (80.26%). P2p core energies level can be attributed to two contributions for phosphate at 134.4 (66.67%) and 135.3 (33.33%) eV for the presence of P2O5 in the sample (Fig. 3D) [42].

P-CDs nanomaterial was deeply studied by solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ss-NMR) through 31P-MAS, 13C CP-MAS, 13C CPPI and 1H-MAS experiments and the results are shown in Fig. 4. It is important to mention that the inherent low sensitivity problem of NMR experiments together with the low density of protons related to the carbon-dots structures makes very difficult the acquisition of the 13C CP-MAS spectra in the solid-state used for the visualization of the hydrocarbon structures at the nanoscale [43]. For instance, the ss-NMR characterization of this kind of materials results in the observation of the organic composition that covers the carbon-dot cores which may explain their interesting fluorescence properties [44].

First, the 31P-MAS spectrum of the P-CDs shows only two resonance signals corresponding to phosphate diester (-0.40 ppm) and phosphate tri-ester (-1.53 ppm) (Fig. 4C) [45,46]. The absent of P2O5 signals clearly indicated that the DHA reacted with the P2O5 molecules (Fig. 4 C and Scheme 1). The information related to the chemical reaction between DHA and P2O5 was extracted from the analysis of the 13C CP-MAS spectrum by observing the different 13C signals of the products that cover the P-CDs (Fig. 4A). The 13C CPPI spectrum edition [47], which shows -CH2- as inverted peaks, was also used to distinguish >CH-, -CH2-, -CH3 and quaternary carbon signals in 13C CP-MAS experiment. The negative peak corresponding to CH2- can be seen in Fig. 4B. Interestingly, the presence of methyl group at a 13C chemical shift of 21.1 ppm shows that the DHA (I) was dehydrated to the pyruvic aldehyde (IV) in the presence of P2O5 (Scheme 1) [48–50] In fact, DHA (Structure I, Scheme 1) is in equilibrium with its hemiacetal dimeric form (Structure III, Scheme 1) [51–53]. While each structure I and IV is in equilibrium with their hydrated structures II and V, respectively, which may explain the complex oxygenated chemical structure that cover the P-CDs particles according to the HRTEM results.

Following the analysis of the ss-NMR results, the other 13C resonance signals are typically carbon atoms bounded to oxygen atoms like alkoxide (signals B, C and D; δ: 61.5 ppm), gem-diol (signals E and E′; δ: 90.3 and 101.8 ppm) [54], hemiacetal (signals F and H; δ: 105.3 and 112.8 ppm)11 and acetal (signal G; δ: 110.8 ppm) carbons [55]. It is important to remark, that there are not signals arising from aromatic or carbonyl carbons at a chemical shift range of 220–120 ppm. With these evidences, the P-CDs particles do not present carbonyl groups coming from the DHA as monomer or another oxidized product. However, the XPS and FTIR results show that carboxylic acid and carbonyl groups were present in the surface of the particles, but their origin may be explained as an conversion of the pyruvic aldehyde to the lactic acid [48,49], as remaining pendant groups, being not representative of the bulk content and that cannot be observed through ss-NMR due to its low concentration in the sample.

With this information, the first step in the synthesis of the covering of the P-CDs was associated with the nucleophilic attack of the hydroxyl-groups of the dimeric DHA to the P2O5 molecules giving rise to a linear like-polymeric structure where the dimeric DHA molecule is bounded to another equivalent structure via phosphate ester giving rise the ‘monomeric structure’. In addition, similar chemical structures for the P-CDs can be proposed according with the information obtained from the ss-NMR results. The structure A (Fig. 4) shows the dimeric structure of the DHA with different degree of functionalization depending on the reactivity of the hydroxyl groups together with the phosphate ester groups. On the other hand, the structure B (Fig. 4) shows an opened form of the dimeric DHA explaining another gem-diol carbon at 101.8 ppm which it was also observed in the 13C CPPI spectrum since it is a quaternary carbon (carbon E′, Fig. 4A-B). Also, the last structure can be explained as a condensation process between monomeric gem-diol form of the DHA and P2O5. Taking in account the polymeric nature of the polyphosphoric acid, a complex phosphate could be formed which derived to the more linear structure VI.

Finally, 1H-MAS spectrum of the P-CDs particles shows different proton regions which were assigned to the hydrogens belonging to the methyl (CH
3 -, δ 1H: 1.4 and 2.4 ppm), alkoxy (-CH
2 -O-, δ 1H: 4–5 ppm) and gem-diol groups (>CH-(OH), δ 1H: 5.2 ppm). The protons of the hydroxyl groups bounded to carbons were tentative assigned at a chemical shift of 3.9 ppm according to previous results in gem-diol compounds [53–55]. Furthermore, a high frequency region between 7 and 9 ppm was associated to the P-OH protons (Fig. 4D) [53]. For the unequivocal assignment of the 1H signals, 2D 1H–13C HETCOR experiment was done and the result is shown in the supporting information section (Fig. SI6).

The indirect 1H dimension extracted from the 2D experiment only shows three groups of signals corresponding to hydrogens bounded to different type of carbons, however, signals at the high frequency region at ∼8 ppm were not observed (Fig. 4E). This fact is in agreement with the efficient reaction of DHA with P2O5 because the reaction of DHA in acid media could have not caused the formation of aromatic compounds. From the comparison of the indirect 1H-dimension and the 1H-MAS spectrum acquired at a MAS rate of 35 kHz (Fig. 4D-E), it was possible to identify unequivocally the P-OH hydrogens (at ∼8 ppm, Fig. 4D), since no correlations were observed even at both short (200 µs) and long contact times (3 ms) between the 13C resonance signals of P-CDs and their protons at high frequency values (>6 ppm) in the 2D HETCOR experiment (Fig. S1). Considering, that the dipolar interactions decrease with the distance between the 1H and 13C increases, the hydrocarbon structure of the P-CDs do not sense the interaction with the P-OH protons and can only be observed with the 1H-MAS spectrum @ 35 kHz (Fig. 4D). Moreover, the 13C nuclei correlated with the protons that were directly linked (correlations A1, B-D and F1, Fig. SI6) or nearby (correlations H2, G2, F2 and E2, Fig. SI5).

The excitation-emission landscapes for P-CDs are shown in Fig. 5A. The 2D projection of the spectra is shown in the inset in Fig. 5A, which is a unique “fingerprint” of the P-CDs. The emission spectrum of P-CDs is broad (Fig. 5A) due to the contribution of many than one spectral component each component originating from the individual emitting molecular species (fluorophores), therefore can be define here the characteristic excitation-dependent emission spectra are identified, indicating the presence of multiple active emission sites within a P-CDs. The excitation spectrum (Fig. 5B, black), obtained for the emission at 480 nm, shows a maximum at 380 nm. The corresponding emission spectrum is shown in the same image (Fig. 5B, red). Measuring fluorescence emission after stepwise excitation at various wavelengths enables detection of all fluorophores in a multi-fluorophore system. To obtain information regarding the number and emission properties of the emitting species contained in the sample, the EEMs are analyzed by using MCR-ALS analysis that performed extraction of the pure spectra of the components, i.e. spectral position and shape. The results of MCR-ALS analysis of the emission profiles for P-CQDs are shown in Fig. 5B.

The emission spectrum contains three components with the maxima 416, 446, 495 nm (Fig. 5B, orange, green and blue). We linked them to the presence of three different fluorophores, indicating surface heterogeneity due to the presence of different oxygen-based groups, such as >C˭O and –COO-. Such groups are confirmed by the XPS, FTIR and NMR spectra. The multicomponent emission spectrum may account for the bright fluorescence properties of this material. It has been observed that as the excitation wavelength increases, a variation in the maximum emission towards red is denoted, which can justify the term carbon quantum dots in this research. The QY of P-CDs at room temperature was calculated to be 12.7% using quinine sulfate as reference (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent; QY = 0.54) as reference.

We verified the effectiveness of fluorescent starch powder as fluorescence labels for the development of latent fingerprints on different types of surfaces. As shown in Fig. 6A-D, P-CDs could successfully stain fingerprints. Fingerprints images obtained with P-CDs displayed well resolved ridges, bifurcations and lakes.

To assess the feasibility of P-CDs as valid system to be considered as alternative to conventional ink staining, was applied the automated fingerprint identification (AFIS), a process by using a computer to match fingerprints against a database of known fingerprints. As example, can be found in Fig. 7 (12 correspondences for every selected image: magnetic band (Fig. 7A) and LCD screen of a smartphone Fig. 7B); of which are labelled in confirming the accuracy of using P-CDs for fingerprints imaging. Issues related to interference background arising from the different matrixes can be circumvented by using these nano-powders, offering some advantages.

The mechanism for staining the fingerprints with P-CDs may include the electrostatic interactions based on the different functional groups on its surface. Since the negative charge on the P-CDs surface, it is suggested that the interaction can be charged typical the basic amino acids presented on the dermal rest of the fingerprints. Must be highlighted that CQDs are not toxic and can be used in an involved fingerprint scene following standard forensic procedures.

In summary, a solid carbon fluorescent nanomaterial was developed in this work, which was synthesized via incorporation of P to form P-CDs by reaction of P2O5 with 1,3 dihydroxyacetone. The material shows excellent emission properties, due to the surface functionalization, containing three types of fluorophores species, which may be linked to the oxygen-based groups on the surface, characterized by ATR NMR and XPS spectroscopies. Fluorescence spectra demonstrated its good emission properties at 495 nm. Thus, the P-CDs nanoparticles could be used for the identification of individuals. We demonstrated the robustness of these P-CDs for fingerprints imaging as promising alternative for potential applications in security-screening needs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank to the projects CTQ2015–68951-C3-3-R (MINECO, Spain), ANPCYT (PICT 2012-0151), Univ. Buenos Aires (UBACyT 2013–2016/043BA) and CONICET (PIP 2014–2016/130), and grant No 173017 (Ministry of Education, Science and Technology of the Republic of Serbia). It is acknowledged the support to COST (ACTION 16101). We also thank SCAI (Málaga, Spain) for performing the XPS and solid NMR measurements. MA thanks also ARDITI-Agência Regional para o Desenvolvimento da Investigação Tecnologia e Inovação is also acknowledged through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-CQM+(Madeira 14–20 Program).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.10.033.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative-qualitative friction ridge analysis: an Introduction to Basic and Advanced Ridgeology

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: G. Saunders, C. Cards In: Proceedings of the 74th Conference of the International Association for Identification, Pensacola, USA.
